text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And thanks for listening and checking out the site.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, that's androidjobs.io.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right, everybody, welcome to today's show.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're going to talk about something that's a little bit out of the ordinary, but very related to software development in general.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that is something known as the GitHub code owners file.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this also is supported on other platforms, other popular Git hosting platforms as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you're using something other than GitHub, you can kind of check out their implementation of how to set up what we're going to talk about today.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's the code owners file.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right, so what is this code owner's file?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Why is it important?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, so let's talk about your code in general, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Early on in Fragmented's history, I believe it was episode 12.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'll put the link in the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Kaushik and I talked about continuous integration and collective code ownership.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the synopsis there is that you should be able to work on any piece of the code inside of the app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: because we all kind of own the same code if it's you know perhaps a messaging or if i'm on the feed if there's tests and continuous integration has been set up i should be able to kind of hop in there and start making changes we all have a collective code ownership in that regard now
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: At some point early on in a company, a startup or a side project or a small company, there's one developer or there's a handful of developers and there's not many people.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So a lot of times you're going to touch all the code at some point in time or you're going to be involved in various different parts of it and you're going to be exposed to it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As your team grows, and this could be for a mobile app, web app, or whatever you're building, you're going to have very different feature sets, and eventually you're probably going to have teams that are responsible for certain features.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For example, you may have a team that's responsible for messaging.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps there's a messaging component in your app that's been built, and there's a team of developers, iOS, Android, web, et cetera, backend, that know everything there is to know about messaging and implementing it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now they may use like web sockets and all different types of technology that's completely different than someone who works on the home feed, which is maybe the, think of like a Facebook or Instagram feed, the main feed of the application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there may be a feed team and all they worry about is the feed, how it's implemented, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You may also have a team that's responsible for a user profile.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You may have another team that's responsible for handling all the payment infrastructure and the payments and anything that generates money for the company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This tends to happen as applications grow large, the companies grow large, they get acquired by larger corporations, and you end up finding that you have anywhere from 30 plus developers to hundreds of developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's just impossible eventually to wrap your entire head around the entire code base because there's just too much going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this is where the code owners file comes into play, which is very useful.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what the code owners file does in really quick high level is it basically gates the pull request to make sure it can't be merged unless it's been approved by a certain person on a team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and how this works is there's a file called code owners it's all capitalized no spaces between it so code owners no extension just code owners and typically in github usually you're going to drop that either in the root of the project or inside the docs or inside of a dot github folder typically i like to just drop it in the root because it's easy to find but there's also a lot of people that like to put it inside of the dot github folder just so it's out of the way now that's up to you how you want to do that
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now the code owners file is just a text file.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And in this text file, you provide a couple of options and it's per line.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can specify users and teams that are responsible for various different files.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now you can use pattern matching.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you can use an asterisk and say asterisk space and say, you know, my username Don Belker, and I'll be responsible for all the files.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what that would mean is if you checked in a file that had been changed that I'm responsible for and you pushed up a PR for it, a pull request, the pull request would automatically be blocked until one of the code owners approved the pull request or all the code owners approved the pull request of whatever had changed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So we'll dig into that in a second.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this is really useful because if you have teams in your GitHub organization and which I recommend, various different people could be in different teams.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this gets really useful because in the code owners file, I can specify paths or I can specify patterns like say all .js files belong to the JavaScript team or
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: all .go files belong to the Go developers, which are maybe only three or four of the developers in the team are experts in Go, so we want to make sure that it's reviewed by them before it's committed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could also have things that are inside of a DevOps folder, and you want to make sure that the DevOps team automatically approves all of those changes that are happening to the DevOps files.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So one of the things that you, so there's different ways you can handle this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And as long as your application is formatted somewhat, you know, organized somewhat well, you're going to have a pretty easy time kind of requesting folks to review the various bits of code that have changed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So this could be, you could be separating it by folder.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could be separating by module.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can be separating by different, you know, styles of separating it however you would like, different packages, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You'll just need to define that pattern inside of the code owner file.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, the other thing that I mentioned here is that in the code owner's file, you can specify who owns what file.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this can be one person, it can be multiple people, it can be teams.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, usually what I recommend you do is apply it to a team because at that point, you can easily add and remove people from a team and you don't have to worry about updating this code owner's file if someone leaves, someone transitions to a different role, et cetera.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if I'm going to have a code owners file and I want to make sure that all of the messaging components are reviewed by the messaging team, I'll create a messaging team inside of GitHub and assign all the developers that are responsible for that into that team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe I'll have another line in the code owners that's responsible for the feed and it goes to the feed team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The same thing for the payments, any of the payment code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, again, payment code might be littered all over the place.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I might have to create a couple of lines in here and make sure I set the teams properly for all of the payment locations.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So, hey, any of these files that match this, make sure that the payments team is requested in the PR.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's kind of walk through what this process looks like.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We've got our code owners file built.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's checked into GitHub.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's good to go.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's got the team set up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We've got our teams built inside of GitHub.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everybody's assigned properly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I'm perhaps, let's just say I'm perhaps part of the messaging team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But for whatever reason, I need to make a change to an upsell that happens inside of a messaging component.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe we drop a message in there and try to get a customer to buy something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I need to change perhaps the border color or the button color for whatever reason the design changed.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So I change it, but that code is actually owned by the payments team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what will happen is as soon as I commit it and push it up to GitHub, GitHub will realize
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don's created a PR.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Looks like he's actually changed a file that matches one of these patterns in the code owners.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Happens to be the payments team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're now going to go ahead and require that someone from the payments team has to approve this PR before it can get merged.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So this is really useful to make sure that you're not breaking someone else's files, you're not doing anything out of the ordinary, or the fact that you just want to make sure you get a second pair of eyes with people that have a subject matter expertise in the area of the code that you've changed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So this can be a good thing and a bad thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: kind of a double-edged sword.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It can be good because it can protect you from making bad decisions and breaking things and making sure you get a good pair of eyes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The other side of the coin is that, well, it can be a bad thing because it can slow you down.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you kind of have to be careful of how you set these things up and it has to be a decision within your team that this is what we would like to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This isn't something I would just throw into an organization without having a discussion with the team about what we're doing and why we're doing it, because it might not be something that's useful at the time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, I do find this very useful as you start growing in team size and there's various components that you want to make sure that are checked in and reviewed by certain people.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And again, maybe you only want, you only carry in the code owners file, like look only in this path, if we change anything in the payment module, then we want to make sure that we are including these, this team of developers to review it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everything else we really don't, we don't care about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I mean, we do care about it, but we don't care that there's a required review.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's no code owner for that particular piece of code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Anybody can approve it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it doesn't require that you have to have all of your code for various code owners, but you can specify, hey, just this package name or this path name here, we want this to make sure that it's reviewed by a certain team.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's pretty much it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a really short episode today.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is known as the code owners file and you can just search code owners.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'll provide a link to this in the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, it's inside of GitHub is where I learned about it and have used it the most, but it is supported by other platforms as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Depending upon what your Git host is, make sure you just kind of look up what their implementation support is for the code owners file and maybe any of the syntax that they have that might be different if there is any difference.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And hopefully that helps you maintain a cleaner code base and prevent future bugs from happening.
